era_context: Late 14th century: Ming Dynasty consolidates power in China; Ottoman Empire expands in the Balkans; Western Europe experiences the Great Schism; the Timurid Renaissance develops in Central Asia; the Joseon Dynasty rules Korea; the Aztec Empire continues expansion in Mesoamerica.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Northern Europe beyond major chronicle centers

events.title: Peasants' Revolt in England
events.description: The Peasants' Revolt, one of the most significant social upheavals in medieval England, erupted in May 1381. Triggered by the poll tax and broader grievances over serfdom and labor conditions, the revolt saw peasants and artisans march on London, led by figures such as Wat Tyler and John Ball. The rebels stormed the Tower of London, executed several royal advisors, and confronted King Richard II. The revolt was ultimately suppressed by royal forces, with Wat Tyler killed during negotiations. The revolt challenged feudal authority and raised questions about social hierarchy, though the immediate outcome was a reassertion of royal power. The long-term impact on English labor relations and social consciousness was significant.
key_figures: John of Gaunt (Duke of Lancaster, royal advisor)

sources.name: Knighton's Chronicle (Henry Knighton, contemporary English chronicler)
Walsingham's Historia Anglicana (Thomas Walsingham, contemporary chronicler)

events.certainty_note: The Peasants' Revolt of 1381 is one of the best-documented events of the 14th century, with multiple contemporary chronicles providing detailed accounts. The date, key figures, and major events are confirmed by independent sources.

events.title: First Battle of Kosovo
events.description: The First Battle of Kosovo (also known as the Battle of the Field of Blackbirds) was fought on 28 June 1389 (not 1381 as sometimes confused in popular sources). However, 1381 marks the period of increasing Ottoman pressure on Serbian lands that would culminate in this battle. Ottoman forces under Murat I were consolidating control over the Balkans, and Serbian resistance was mounting. The year 1381 represents the escalation of tensions that would lead to the decisive 1389 battle.
events.certainty_note: The First Battle of Kosovo is traditionally dated to 1389, not 1381. This entry reflects the period of Ottoman-Serbian conflict that characterized the 1380s and culminated in the 1389 battle. The year 1381 is assigned to mark the escalation of tensions rather than a specific battle.

events.title: Ottoman Expansion in the Balkans
events.description: Ottoman forces under Murat I continued their systematic expansion in the Balkans, consolidating control over Bulgarian and Serbian territories. In 1381, the Ottomans were engaged in campaigns to subjugate remaining independent Balkan principalities and to establish Ottoman administrative control. These campaigns were part of the broader Ottoman expansion that would eventually lead to the conquest of Constantinople in 1453.
events.certainty_note: Ottoman expansion in the Balkans in 1381 is documented in later Ottoman chronicles. The specific campaigns of 1381 are not always clearly distinguished from surrounding years.

events.title: Timur's Consolidation of Power in Central Asia
events.description: Timur (Tamerlane) continued to consolidate his power in Central Asia, establishing Samarkand as his capital and center of the Timurid Renaissance. In 1381, he was engaged in campaigns to secure his control over the Silk Road trade routes and to establish his authority over rival Central Asian powers. These efforts laid the foundation for his later expansion into the Middle East and beyond.

sources.name: Sharaf al-Din Ali Yazdi, Zafarnama (biography of Timur, written early 15th century)

events.certainty_note: Timur's consolidation of power in Central Asia in 1381 is documented in later biographical sources. The specific events of 1381 are not always clearly distinguished from surrounding years.

events.title: Ming Dynasty Consolidation
events.description: The Ming Dynasty under the Hongwu Emperor continued to consolidate central authority and eliminate remaining Yuan Dynasty remnants. In 1381, the Ming government was engaged in reorganizing provincial administration, establishing the examination system for civil service, and strengthening the imperial bureaucracy. These reforms were crucial to the stability and longevity of the Ming Dynasty.
events.certainty_note: Ming administrative consolidation in 1381 is documented in official records. The specific reforms of this year are part of a broader process of consolidation that occurred throughout the 1370s and 1380s.

events.title: Wycliffe's Continued Reformist Activity
events.description: John Wycliffe, the English theologian and reformer, continued his advocacy for church reform and the translation of the Bible into English. In 1381, he was in his final years of life (he died in 1384). His ideas were spreading through his followers, the Lollards, who would continue his reformist agenda after his death. Wycliffe's work challenged papal authority and advocated for a more accessible, vernacular Christianity.

sources.name: Knighton's Chronicle (Henry Knighton, contemporary English chronicler)

events.certainty_note: Wycliffe's continued reformist activity in 1381 is documented in his own writings and in contemporary chronicles. The date is confirmed.

disconfirming_evidence: The First Battle of Kosovo is traditionally dated to 1389, not 1381. This entry reflects the period of Ottoman-Serbian conflict that characterized the 1380s, but the specific battle did not occur in 1381. Some modern historians have questioned the historical accuracy of certain details in the Peasants' Revolt as reported by contemporary chroniclers, particularly regarding the exact sequence of events and the motivations of the rebels. However, the basic fact of the revolt and its major events are confirmed by multiple sources. No major disconfirming evidence undermines the significance of the events listed.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1381 is rich, particularly for England and Europe. The Peasants' Revolt is one of the best-documented events of the 14th century, with multiple contemporary chronicles providing detailed accounts. Chinese sources are also abundant for this period. Ottoman and Middle Eastern sources are more fragmentary and often written decades after the events. Sub-Saharan Africa, the Americas, and Oceania are essentially undocumented. The surviving record is heavily weighted toward political and military events; economic, social, and cultural developments are underrepresented. English chronicles may reflect court biases and the perspectives of the literate elite.

graph_edges.note: The Peasants' Revolt of 1381 led to its suppression in 1382.
Ottoman-Serbian tensions in 1381 escalated to the First Battle of Kosovo in 1389.
Wycliffe's reformist activity in 1381 continued until his death in 1384.
